a: A prompt is the text you give an AI model to tell it what you want. It can be a question, an instruction, or some text plus a request, and the model writes its answer based on it.
fs: A prompt is the full input you send the model; the clearer and more specific it is, the more reliable the output.
fj: Thinks a prompt is just a short question, so they give vague one-liners and are surprised when the answer misses what they wanted.

q: What is the difference between a system prompt and a user prompt?
a: The system prompt sets the rules and role for the AI (like 'You are a helpful tutor, keep answers short'). The user prompt is the actual question or task you type each time. The system prompt stays in the background and shapes how every user message is answered.
fs: System prompt = persistent rules/persona with higher priority; user prompt = the per-turn task. Put behavior in the system, the request in the user.
fj: Crams role, rules, and the question all into one user message, so the model's behavior is inconsistent from turn to turn.

code.label: two roles
lines: system: You are a concise SQL tutor.
user: Explain what GROUP BY does.

a: A token is a small chunk of text the model reads and writes in, usually a short word or part of a word. The model does not see whole sentences at once; it processes them one token at a time. Both your prompt and the model's answer are counted in tokens.
fs: A token is the model's unit of text (~3/4 of a word in English); cost, speed, and context limits are all measured in tokens.
fj: Assumes one token equals one word, so they badly mis-estimate how long a prompt or answer really is.

a: Few-shot prompting means showing the model a few examples of the task done correctly, right inside your prompt, before asking it to do a new one. The examples teach it the format and style you want. Giving zero examples is called zero-shot.
fs: Few-shot adds a handful of input/output demos so the model copies the pattern; great when you need a specific format or tone.
fj: Describes the task in words only and never shows an example, then wonders why the output format keeps drifting.

a: Temperature is a setting that controls how random or creative the model's answers are. A low value (near 0) makes it pick the most likely, predictable words, good for facts and code. A higher value (around 0.7-1.0) makes it more varied and creative, good for brainstorming or writing.
fs: Temperature scales output randomness: near 0 for deterministic, repeatable tasks; higher for creative variety. Start at 0 while testing.
fj: Thinks higher temperature means 'smarter' answers, when it really just means more random and less repeatable.
